mappings.rationale: Essential for understanding the philosophical foundations of statehood, sovereignty, social contract, authority, and human rights (A, A.4, C.2).
Broadly addresses political theory including the state, democracy, justice, and the exercise of political power (A, B, C.1).
Surveys the historical development of political theories from ancient to modern times, spanning all outline topics.
Popular sovereignty, consent of the governed, and democratic governance as a basis of legitimacy are central themes (A.4.a, A.5).
Directly addresses the role of ideology in political systems and the relationship between ideological and civil politics (C.3.a).
Human rights as a political concept are the central subject (C.2).
Examines anarchism as a named political ideology (C.3.b.i).
Explores communism as a named political ideology and its practical applications (C.3.b.ii).
Marx's political ideology -- including alienation, capitalism critique, and dialectical materialism -- is foundational (C.3.b.vi).
Fascism as a named political ideology is the central subject (C.3.b.iv).
Liberalism as a named political ideology is examined in its historical and contemporary forms (C.3.b.v).
Socialism as a named political ideology is explored from its utopian origins to modern social democracy (C.3.b.viii).
Nationalism as an ideology and its relationship to the national state are central themes (A, C.3.b.vii).
Populism as a contemporary political ideology, treating sovereignty and democratic power, is examined (C.3.b).
Neoliberalism as a current political ideology affecting state-market relations is explored (C.3.b).
Hobbes's social contract and state of nature remain foundational to theories of political authority (A.4.b).
Locke's theories of consent, natural rights, and constitutional government shaped modern liberal political thought (A.4.a, A.5.a).
Rousseau's social contract, popular sovereignty, and citizenship theory are foundational to modern political thought (A.4.a, A.4.b).
Tocqueville's democratic theory, political science, and analysis of public opinion's influence on governance remain influential (B.4).
Mill's liberal political theory on individual liberty, representative government, and political participation is essential reading (A.2, C.3.b.v).
Machiavelli's political theory about the concept of political power and statecraft remains a touchstone (C.1).
Arendt's theories of political action, power, totalitarianism, and constitutionalism are explored (B, C.1).
Aristotle's foundational political theory including forms of government and political science informs the entire tradition (A.4, A.5).
The relationship between the state and the individual, political participation, and rights are examined (A.2, C.2).
Freedom of expression as a human right and its role in public opinion are central themes (B.4, C.2).
The rule of law as a feature of constitutional government is the central subject (A.5.b).
The origins of constitutional government and limits on monarchical authority are traced from this foundational document (A.5.a).
Addresses the problem of centralization vs. decentralization of power (C.4.d).
Authoritarian government as a political system contrasting with constitutional governance is examined (A.4, A.5).
Directly addresses the problem of church and state separation (C.4.a).
The relationship between religion and politics bears directly on the church-state problem (C.4.a).
Revolutionary political action as a means of adapting political forms to changing conditions is examined (C.4.e).
Political action by militant groups using violence as a form of political engagement is the central subject (B.3).
Explores how political authority is exercised by states and international organizations (A.3, C.4.c).
Feminist political theory challenges traditional political structures and advances rights (C.2, C.3.b).
Political tolerance as a principle of liberal governance and the church-state relationship is examined (C.4.a).
Utopian political theory and visions of ideal political organization are explored (C.3).
The national state in the international community, including state sovereignty and political authority, is examined (A.3).
